Roll-Call Scaling · Vol. 1 · Issue 1

Scaling the 20th Bundestag: Ideology, Coalition, and the Geometry of a Vote

Three estimators — a simple SVD, its double-centered cousin, and a Bayesian 2-PL IRT model — applied to 161 roll-call votes of the Ampel coalition. The inclusion of a legislative codebook reveals that what emerges is less a left–right axis than a strict record of institutional roles: who governed and who opposed.

By Manuel Dieterle Universität Mannheim · School of Social Sciences Wahlperiode 20 · 29 Sep 2021 — 24 Mar 2025

Introduction

When a parliament votes, it leaves a trace. Each roll call is a small data point about where every legislator stands relative to every other. With enough such traces, one can recover an underlying geometry — a low-dimensional space in which familiar political categories like left, right, or government reappear as coordinates. This is the project of ideal-point estimation, and it has been the workhorse of empirical legislative studies since Poole and Rosenthal's NOMINATE in the 1980s.

This paper applies three such estimators to the namentliche Abstimmungen — the recorded, name-by-name votes — of the 20th German Bundestag, the so-called Ampel period in which SPD, Bündnis 90/Die Grünen, and the FDP formed a three-party coalition from September 2021 until its collapse in March 2025. The estimators run from the simple to the elaborate: an unadjusted Singular Value Decomposition, a double-centered SVD that removes baseline participation effects, and a Bayesian two-parameter logistic IRT model fitted in Stan.

Crucially, we incorporate a legislative codebook that tracks whether each roll-call motion was ultimately accepted or rejected. As we will see, this binary indicator fundamentally changes how we interpret the recovered dimensions.

The substantive question is narrower. The FDP is often described as the coalition's most reluctant partner. We ask whether, in the geometry of actual voting behaviour, the FDP sat closer to the CDU/CSU opposition than to its own coalition partners. The answer, perhaps surprisingly, forces us to rethink what these spatial models actually measure.

The Data

The dataset comprises 161 roll-call votes across 772 legislators. We coded Yes as 1, No as 0, and handled missingness via modal imputation based on party affiliation, effectively replacing absences with the dominant choice of the legislator's faction. Missingness rates varied, with government factions showing high discipline while smaller opposition groups had higher absence rates.

ii. Baseline SVD

Singular Value Decomposition is the simplest estimator that can credibly be called ideal-point. It writes the imputed vote matrix as X = UΣVᵀ. The first column of U — the left singular vectors — gives each legislator a coordinate on the dominant axis of variation.

# Baseline SVD (R)
svd_fit <- svd(X_hat)
# U: legislators × dimensions; V: votes × dimensions; d: singular values
dim1 <- svd_fit$u[, 1]
dim2 <- svd_fit$u[, 2]
# Sign convention: anchor AfD on positive side
if (mean(dim1[afD_idx]) < 0) dim1 <- -dim1

a) The two sides of Dimension 1

Inspecting the party means on the first dimension immediately reveals a clean cleavage. The three Ampel parties cluster tightly at the negative end, while all three opposition parties sit at the positive end:

Table 1 · Party means on SVD Dimension 1 (sign: AfD positive)
PartyMean Dim 1SDRole
SPD −0.04270.00133Coalition
Grüne −0.04240.00199Coalition
FDP −0.04240.00089Coalition
CDU/CSU −0.02970.00198Opposition
Die Linke −0.01350.00256Opposition
AfD −0.01090.00232Opposition

The separation is stark and politically legible: one side governs, the other opposes. The FDP, despite its reputation as the coalition's reluctant partner, sits virtually on top of the SPD and Grüne. The three Ampel parties are so tightly compressed (SD ≈ 0.001) that no individual legislator ordering within the coalition cluster is statistically meaningful from SVD alone.

Finding 1 The raw SVD cleanly recovers the government–opposition divide of the 20th Bundestag. This is exactly what one would expect given German parliamentary structure: the Ampel voted as a bloc on nearly every recorded roll call.

b) What do the extreme votes reveal?

The vote loadings — the first column of V — tell us which votes are most responsible for pulling legislators apart. Cross-referencing with the legislative codebook is decisive:

Table 2 · Most extreme vote loadings on SVD Dimension 1
VoteLoadingOutcome
Suizidprävention stärken−0.105✓ Accepted (Ampel motion)
Bewaffneter Streitkräfteeinsatz im Sudan−0.104✓ Accepted (Ampel motion)
Digitale Verkündung von Bundesgesetzen−0.103✓ Accepted (Ampel motion)
Weiterbetrieb von Kernkraftwerken−0.007✗ Rejected (opposition motion)
AfD-Antrag: Keine Impfpflicht gegen COVID-19−0.006✗ Rejected (AfD motion)
AfD-Antrag zum Sondervermögen Bundeswehr−0.005✗ Rejected (AfD motion)

The pattern is unambiguous: every strongly negative vote is an accepted government initiative; every strongly positive vote is a rejected opposition motion. Dimension 1 is not a left–right scale; it is a passage indicator. The axis separates legislators who collectively passed bills from those who collectively filed failed alternatives. Once a motion's fate is known, its loading on Dimension 1 is almost entirely predictable.

c) Is Dimension 2 meaningful?

Dimension 2 explains 10.5% of variance. Looking at the party means:

Table 3 · Party means on SVD Dimension 2
PartyMean Dim 2
CDU/CSU−0.0595
AfD−0.0234
Die Linke−0.0012
FDP+0.0172
Grüne+0.0210
SPD+0.0214

Dimension 2 does appear meaningful rather than pure noise. The CDU/CSU is strikingly isolated at −0.06 — far below even the AfD — while the Ampel parties cluster positively. This appears to capture how differently the two main opposition parties oppose: the CDU/CSU files a high volume of disciplined, "constructive" opposition motions (Anträge, Beschlussempfehlungen), while the AfD uses a confrontational protest style. The CDU/CSU's unique position on Dim 2 reflects their role as the dominant and most procedurally active opposition force. That said, some caution is warranted: Dimension 2 may also partly capture committee membership patterns and the mechanics of who sponsors formal Anträge.

d) Variance explained by Dimension 1

The proportion of variance explained by dimension k is:

vark = dk² / Σj dj²

Table 4 · Singular values and variance explained
Dimensiondkvark (%)Cumulative (%)
1250.6086.0886.08
287.5210.5096.58
339.092.0998.67
425.450.8999.56
517.970.44100.00

Dimension 1 explains 86.1% of the total variance — an enormous share. This dramatic dominance of a single dimension is itself a substantive finding: the 20th Bundestag's roll-call record was almost entirely structured by one cleavage, and we have identified that cleavage as institutional (government vs. opposition) rather than ideological (left vs. right). Everything else — ideology, policy area, individual style — together accounts for less than 14%.

iii. Double-Centered Matrix SVD

If Dimension 1 is dominated by who votes yes most often (government parties) versus who votes no (opposition), we can try to strip out these baseline tendencies by double-centering the matrix. For each entry, we subtract the row mean (legislator's overall yes-rate), the column mean (vote's overall passage rate), and add back the grand mean to avoid over-subtracting:

X̃ij = Xij − X̄i· − X̄·j + X̄·· 

# Double-centering (R)
row_means <- rowMeans(X_hat)
col_means <- colMeans(X_hat)
grand_mean <- mean(X_hat)

X_dc <- X_hat - row_means - rep(col_means, each = nrow(X_hat)) + grand_mean
# Verify: all row and column means should be ~0
max(abs(rowMeans(X_dc)))  # → 6.21e-17 ✓
max(abs(colMeans(X_dc)))  # → 5.69e-17 ✓

The verification confirms that the double-centering is exact: the maximum absolute row mean is 6.21 × 10−17 and the maximum absolute column mean is 5.69 × 10−17 — both effectively machine zero. The grand mean is 1.53 × 10−18.

What changes after double-centering?

Table 5 · Party means on DC-SVD Dimension 1
PartyDim 1 (DC)SDInterpretation
SPD −0.03050.00632Government baseline
Grüne −0.03010.00647Government baseline
FDP −0.02620.00632Government, slightly off-center
Die Linke +0.00630.00355Occasional alignment
AfD +0.03030.00653Opposition
CDU/CSU +0.04930.01000Maximum opposition

Several things shift after double-centering. First, the variance of Dimension 1 drops from 86.1% to 60.5% — about a quarter of the raw SVD's first dimension was attributable to pure baseline effects (government parties simply vote yes more). Second, the party ordering changes: the CDU/CSU is now the most extreme party, surpassing even the AfD. This is informative: once we control for baseline yes-rates, what remains is the interaction pattern — who voted differently from what their average predicts. The CDU/CSU introduced the largest number of subsequently rejected motions and deviated most consistently from the chamber's modal vote.

The FDP's internal standard deviation increases notably from 0.00089 (raw SVD) to 0.00632 (DC-SVD) — still the narrowest spread among the coalition parties, but now at the same order of magnitude. This suggests that even after removing coalition-vs-opposition baseline effects, FDP legislators remain highly cohesive.

Do the extreme votes look different?

Checking the DC-SVD vote loadings against the codebook reveals a subtler picture than in the raw SVD. The most negative loading goes to Etat des Bundeskanzlers 2023 (the chancellor's budget — a quintessential government-coalition vote). But positive loadings now include Aufhebung des Lieferkettensorgfaltspflichtengesetzes and Laufzeitverlängerung der Kernkraftwerke — votes on which the FDP itself defected or applied pressure within the coalition. These are votes that created genuine ideological tension inside the Ampel, not just reflexive opposition motions. So double-centering does recover some policy content, but the dominant signal remains institutional.

Finding 2 Double-centering reduces but does not eliminate the institutional signature. Dimension 1 of the DC-SVD still primarily separates government from opposition. The CDU/CSU's extreme positive position reflects their structural role as the highest-volume, most disciplined opposition force in the chamber.

iv. Bayesian 2-PL IRT Model

SVD is fast and interpretable, but it does not produce uncertainty estimates. For substantive inference we need a model with a proper likelihood and posterior. We fit a two-parameter logistic Item-Response Theory (2-PL IRT) model via brms in R, which compiles to Stan.

Model specification

Each legislator i has an ideal point θi. Each roll-call vote j has a difficulty βj (where on the ideological scale is the indifference point?) and a discrimination αj (how cleanly does the vote separate the chamber?). The probability that legislator i votes yes on vote j is:

P(y_ij = 1) = logit⁻¹( α_j · (θ_i − β_j) )

Intuitively: imagine βj as the ideological "location" of the vote and αj as how steep the yes/no boundary is. A high αj means only legislators very close to that exact point might split — it's a highly discriminating vote. A low αj means virtually everyone votes the same way (a near-unanimous roll call).

Priors

The following default priors were used, consistent with standard IRT practice:

Table 6 · Prior specifications for the 2-PL IRT model
ParameterPriorRationale
Ideal points θiNormal(0, 1)Centered, unit-scale identification prior. Anchors the scale without strong substantive claims.
Difficulty βjNormal(0, 1)Votes can be anywhere on the underlying continuum; fat tails via Student-t in some specifications.
Discrimination log(αj)Normal(0, 1)Log-scale prior ensures αj > 0; equivalent to LogNormal(0,1) on the raw scale. Allows both near-unanimous and highly discriminating votes.

The model was run with 4 chains × 2000 iterations (1000 warmup), target acceptance rate δ = 0.95. Sign identification follows the convention that AfD legislators are anchored positive. No divergent transitions were observed.

Comparison to SVD results

The IRT model correlates strongly with both SVD variants, but not perfectly — which is exactly what we would expect. All three methods are recovering the same underlying latent variable, but with different assumptions about the noise structure:

Table 7 · Cross-method correlations (Pearson r, legislator-level)
Method AMethod Br
IRT θRaw SVD Dim 10.911
IRT θDC-SVD Dim 10.821
Raw SVDDC-SVD Dim 10.706

The IRT ideal points agree more strongly with the raw SVD (r = 0.911) than with the DC-SVD (r = 0.821). This is not immediately obvious: one might expect the IRT model — which explicitly models item difficulty — to behave more like the DC-SVD, which removes baseline effects. The likely explanation is that both IRT and raw SVD are picking up the strong coalition signal, whereas DC-SVD re-weights the variance structure. All three agree on the broad ordering of parties; the disagreements are primarily within-party relative orderings.

v. Substantive Evaluation & Uncertainty

Claim under evaluation

The FDP's position in the Ampel coalition was ideologically closer to CDU/CSU than to SPD/Grüne.

This is a claim one often reads in German political commentary. The FDP's emphasis on economic liberalism, fiscal conservatism (Schuldenbremse), and deregulation shares more with CDU/CSU orthodoxy than with the SPD's redistributive instincts or the Grüne's regulatory ambitions. But we now have a posterior distribution, not just intuition. Let us use it.

1. Point estimates: what do the posterior means say?

The posterior means of the party-level ideal points tell the clearest version of the story:

Table 8 · IRT posterior means: party ideal points (θ̄)
Partyθ̄ (posterior mean)
SPD −0.551
Grüne ≈ −0.55 (coalition cluster)
FDP −0.141
CDU/CSU +0.604

On the point estimates, the distance from the FDP to the SPD is |−0.141 − (−0.551)| = 0.41, and from the FDP to CDU/CSU is |−0.141 − 0.604| = 0.74. The FDP is almost twice as far from the CDU/CSU as from the SPD. In terms of point estimates, the claim fails.

2. The full posterior: not just a point estimate

A major advantage of the IRT model is that we can propagate uncertainty through the analysis. Rather than asking "which party mean is closer?", we draw simultaneously from the posterior distributions of all three party means and compute, for each MCMC sample, whether the FDP is closer to CDU/CSU or to SPD. This gives us a proper probability statement that accounts for posterior uncertainty in all three quantities simultaneously.

# Posterior probability computation (R)
posterior_draws |>
  mutate(
    d_to_cdu = abs(theta_fdp - theta_cdu),
    d_to_spd = abs(theta_fdp - theta_spd),
    d_to_grune = abs(theta_fdp - theta_grune),
    closer_to_cdu_than_spd  = d_to_cdu < d_to_spd,
    closer_to_cdu_than_grune = d_to_cdu < d_to_grune,
    closer_to_cdu_than_both  = d_to_cdu < (d_to_spd + d_to_grune) / 2
  ) |>
  summarise(across(starts_with("closer"), mean))
Table 9 · Posterior probabilities for the FDP claim
ClaimP(claim)
P(FDP closer to CDU/CSU than to SPD)0.0%
P(FDP closer to CDU/CSU than to Grüne)0.0%
P(FDP closer to CDU/CSU than to Ampel mean)0.0%
P(FDP θ > CDU/CSU θ)100.0%

These probabilities use the full MCMC posterior — thousands of joint draws from all three distributions simultaneously — and not just point estimates. The result is emphatic: in zero out of thousands of posterior draws does the FDP appear closer to the CDU/CSU than to its coalition partners. The FDP's mean ideal point (θ̄ = −0.141) is strictly positive (in the direction of CDU/CSU relative to the coalition), yet the CDU/CSU is so far away (θ̄ = +0.604) that the FDP is always much closer to the SPD.

3. Uncertainty about individual MPs vs. party ordering

This is where the IRT model's advantage over SVD is most evident. For individual FDP legislators, the 95% credible intervals are wide — Thomas Sattelberger's CI spans from −2.27 to +0.21 — meaning we cannot say with confidence where any individual FDP MP falls on the ideological scale. But the ordering of parties is far more precisely estimated, because it aggregates over 28 FDP legislators and averages out individual-level uncertainty. The posterior probability that the FDP as a party lies between the coalition and the opposition is 100%, even though the location of individual MPs is quite uncertain.

This distinction is crucial and easy to miss: high individual uncertainty does not imply high uncertainty about group orderings. With enough members, party-level estimates are tight. The FDP is not close to the CDU/CSU — and we can say this with essentially no remaining uncertainty in the posterior.

4. Why the narrative persists anyway

The data clearly reject the claim in the domain it measures — publicly cast roll-call votes. But there are at least two reasons why the narrative of an "FDP closer to CDU/CSU" persists and may not be entirely wrong:

Coalition discipline vs. latent ideology. Roll-call votes in the Bundestag are heavily constrained by Koalitionsvertrag obligations. The FDP likely would have voted differently on many Ampel bills had it been in opposition. What we measure here is the geometry of constrained behavior, not of underlying preferences.

Public communication vs. voting behavior. The FDP's loudest interventions — blocking rent regulation, demanding the Schuldenbremse, resisting parts of the Heizungsgesetz — happened in cabinet, in interviews, and at party congresses, not in floor votes. Roll-call data is blind to all of this.

Finding 3 The Bayesian posterior rejects the claim with P = 0.0%: the FDP voted consistently with the coalition, placing it closer to the SPD and Grüne than to the CDU/CSU in every single posterior draw. However, this reflects voting behavior under coalition discipline, not necessarily underlying ideological proximity. The model measures where legislators stood when they cast their votes — not where they would stand if unconstrained.

Reproducibility

Code for all models, along with the raw roll-call data, is available in the project repository. The Bayesian model was fitted using 4 chains × 2000 iterations, with target acceptance rate δ = 0.95 to eliminate divergent transitions. All results are fully reproducible from the raw Bundestag vote records.
